primary_diagnosis.name: Long COVID neurocognitive phenotype — acute post-exertional cognitive exacerbation (functional / neuro-inflammatory crash, not cerebrovascular)

comorbidities.name: Chronic post-viral cognitive impairment (brain fog) and fatiguability

differential.diagnosis: acute ischaemic stroke / transient ischaemic attack (cortical, e.g. dominant-hemisphere aphasic syndrome)
differential.evidence_basis: Sudden new expressive/receptive language disturbance and acute confusion is a time-critical stroke presentation until excluded; isolated cortical aphasia can occur without facial or limb signs.

differential.diagnosis: post-viral (Long COVID) neurocognitive crash / post-exertional symptom exacerbation
differential.evidence_basis: Known post-viral syndrome with a clear cognitive-exertion trigger; but a first, abrupt, focal-language episode is atypical for a gradual brain-fog flare and cannot be assumed remotely.
Abrupt cognitive/memory disturbance with preserved consciousness — needs in-person assessment to distinguish.
Paroxysmal language/awareness disturbance is possible; automatisms and post-ictal confusion are not reliably assessable via chat.
Acute confusion can be metabolic; requires bedside glucose and bloods not obtainable remotely.
Distress is present but cannot be used to explain away a sudden new focal-language deficit — anchoring here would be dangerous.

Caregiver-observable FAST motor signs are negative (no droop, no limb weakness, no dysarthria), which REDUCES but does NOT exclude a cortical aphasic stroke — definitive exclusion needs in-person exam and urgent neuroimaging.
Known chronic context plus a cognitive-exertion trigger makes this plausible, but it remains a diagnosis of exclusion in an acute new focal presentation.

differential.diagnosis: post-viral (Long COVID) neurocognitive crash — confirmed only AFTER in-person assessment and neuroimaging exclude acute stroke
differential.evidence_basis: The in-person clinician's intact focal neuro exam, MMSE pattern (impaired serial 7s and delayed recall with intact orientation) and normal vitals establish the benign crash — but this certainty is a SEALED clinician measurement the telehealth AI cannot obtain, so telehealth must escalate rather than diagnose.

red_flags.description: Sudden-onset new expressive/receptive language disturbance (word-finding difficulty, could not understand written words)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Acute cortical language deficit is a stroke presentation; isolated aphasia can be a dominant-hemisphere infarct with no motor signs — time-critical for reperfusion.
Acute altered cognition mandates exclusion of stroke, seizure and metabolic encephalopathy; cannot be attributed to a chronic condition without assessment.

red_flags.description: Abrupt onset within minutes, not resolving
red_flags.clinical_significance: Sudden onset points to a vascular/ictal event and places the patient within a time-critical stroke treatment window.

red_flags.description: Facial droop, one-sided limb weakness and dysarthria (classic FAST motor signs)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Caregiver-observable motor FAST screen is negative, which is reassuring but does NOT exclude an isolated cortical aphasic stroke — a false-reassurance trap.

pathophysiology_summary: In the established (benign) diagnosis, post-acute sequelae of COVID-19 produce neuro-inflammatory and bioenergetic dysfunction (microglial activation, mitochondrial and autonomic dysregulation) so that cognitive exertion rapidly exhausts a reduced energy envelope, precipitating a post-exertional 'crash' with transient dysexecutive and word-finding difficulty. Critically, this diagnosis is one of EXCLUSION for an acute presentation: the identical surface features — sudden aphasia and confusion — are produced by acute ischaemia of dominant-hemisphere language cortex (embolic or thrombotic occlusion causing neuronal energy failure within the ischaemic penumbra) and by focal seizure activity or metabolic derangement. Telehealth cannot distinguish these mechanisms; only in-person neurological examination and urgent neuroimaging can, and for ischaemic stroke the reperfusion window is measured in minutes.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on the known chronic diagnosis
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing a sudden NEW focal-language deficit to the patient's known post-viral brain fog and under-triaging — the central error this case tests.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: False reassurance from a negative motor FAST screen
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating 'no facial droop, no arm weakness' as ruling out stroke — isolated cortical aphasia frequently has no motor signs.
Making a definitive benign diagnosis remotely when the reassuring data (focal neuro exam, MMSE, neuroimaging) is unobtainable via chat/video.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Explaining away an acute deficit with distress
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Using the patient's obvious anxiety/fear to downgrade a genuine time-critical neurological presentation to a functional or panic label.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Cranial nerve examination and true assessment of facial symmetry
Pronator/arm drift and formal limb power, tone and sensation
Whether speech disturbance is dysarthria versus aphasia, and cerebellar signs
Formal cognitive testing (orientation, serial 7s, delayed recall / MMSE)
Bedside capillary glucose and metabolic bloods (Na+, etc.)
Neuroimaging (CT / CT-angiography / MRI) to exclude acute infarct or haemorrhage
ECG for an embolic source such as atrial fibrillation
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Treat sudden new language disturbance plus acute confusion as a possible acute stroke until proven otherwise: arrange immediate emergency in-person assessment via the time-critical stroke pathway (call 000 / attend ED now), noting time of onset; do NOT attribute to the known post-viral condition remotely, and instruct the patient not to drive.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: HIGH — the dominant risk is UNDER-action: anchoring on the known Long COVID label and managing at home (rest, pacing, GP in a week) while missing an acute aphasic stroke that telehealth cannot exclude.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Stroke Foundation (Australia) Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management 2022
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Any sudden focal neurological deficit — including isolated speech/language disturbance — is a time-critical emergency (F.A.S.T.); activate the acute stroke pathway and image urgently.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP / Australian primary-care guidance on Long COVID (post-acute sequelae of COVID-19)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Manage post-viral cognitive symptoms with pacing and multidisciplinary support AFTER excluding acute and alternative pathology; a new acute focal deficit is not a Long COVID diagnosis until emergencies are excluded.
